result:
The concept of "time" is perhaps the most deceptive construct humanity has ever invented. We treat it like a river—something that flows in a singular, immutable direction, carrying us from the cradle to the grave—but our perception of it is entirely subjective. Think about the way a summer afternoon in childhood felt like an infinite expanse, a sprawling, golden eternity where the sun seemed glued to the horizon. Now, in the haze of adulthood, those same hours are merely a blur, a frantic shedding of moments as we race toward deadlines, appointments, and the inevitable folding of the evening into the next morning.

We are creatures of rhythm, yet we spend our lives fighting the tempo. We are biologically tethered to the rotation of the planet—the circadian pulse of light and dark—but we have built a civilization that demands we ignore the sun. We light up our nights with screens and artificial radiance, extending our reach into the shadows, tricking our brains into believing that the day never truly ends. In doing so, we have sacrificed the sanctity of the "in-between." We have lost the ability to sit still without the itching need for input.

Consider the silence. True silence is almost impossible to find anymore. Even in the deepest woods, there is the hum of a distant airplane or the persistent buzz of an insect, but beyond the physical noise, there is the mental noise. We carry the world in our pockets. We have instant access to the sum total of human knowledge, and yet we seem less wise, more fractured, and increasingly anxious. We are perpetually "plugged in," tethered to a global consciousness that thrives on outrage and immediacy. We have forgotten how to sit with a single, unadulterated thought. We have forgotten how to be bored, and in losing boredom, we have lost the incubator of creativity.

Creativity, at its core, is a result of synthesis—of taking disparate threads of experience and weaving them into something new. But synthesis requires processing time. It requires a fallow field where ideas can lie dormant, germinating under the surface of the subconscious. If we never stop consuming—if we are always reading, watching, listening, or scrolling—we never give the soil time to rest. We are constantly planting new seeds in a field that has no time to grow. We become collectors of information rather than architects of wisdom.

This brings me to the idea of "digital friction." We are currently living through a transition in human history that is as significant as the invention of the printing press or the steam engine. The internet has dissolved the friction of distance, making the entire world a neighborhood. But in removing friction, we have removed the value of the journey. When you can have anything, learn anything, or see anyone at the press of a button, the act of "getting there" loses its texture. The struggle is where the meaning lives. The hike to the mountain peak is what makes the view from the top feel like a triumph. If we were simply teleported to the summit, we might appreciate the scenery, but we wouldn’t feel the weight of the achievement.

Perhaps this is why there is such a resurgence in "analog" hobbies. People are returning to vinyl records, fountain pens, sourdough baking, and gardening. It’s a quiet rebellion against the frictionless efficiency of the modern age. There is a profound satisfaction in the resistance of the physical world—the way flour coats your hands, the way a needle catches the groove of a record, the way a plant grows at its own agonizingly slow pace. These are not efficient tasks. They are inefficient by design. They force us to re-enter the temporal reality of the physical world, to acknowledge that some things simply cannot be rushed.

Nature remains the ultimate teacher in this regard. Trees do not rush to grow, and yet they reach the sky. They deal with droughts, storms, and the slow turn of the seasons without anxiety. They exist in a state of "being," whereas we are trapped in a state of "becoming." We are always looking for the next promotion, the next purchase, the next stage of life. We treat our present lives as a waiting room for a future version of ourselves that we imagine will finally be satisfied. But the future is a mirage. By the time we arrive at the "better" version of our lives, our goalposts will have shifted. We will be looking toward the next horizon, forever missing the grace of the current moment.

There is a liberation in accepting that we are small, fleeting, and largely insignificant in the cosmic timeline. If you look up at the stars on a clear night—really look at them, past the atmospheric veil—you realize that the light hitting your eyes began its journey thousands or millions of years ago. You are looking into the deep past. In the face of that scale, our daily frustrations—the email we forgot to send, the awkward comment we made at dinner, the slight we felt yesterday—evaporate. They are dust motes in a cathedral. 

This insignificance isn't depressing; it’s a relief. It means the pressure to "be somebody" or to "make a mark" is self-imposed. We are free to simply exist, to enjoy the texture of the air, the flavor of a meal, the sound of a voice we love. We are free to be kind, not because it builds our legacy, but because kindness is the only sensible response to the shared fragility of the human condition. We are all, after all, just stardust held together by gravity for a very short while, stumbling through the dark, trying to figure out the map while we walk the path.

Maybe the secret to living well is not to find a grand purpose, but to cultivate a sense of wonder. To treat the world like a child who has just discovered that fireflies glow and that rain makes puddles. If we can maintain that capacity for awe, the world ceases to be a machine we have to navigate and becomes a miracle we are privileged to witness. 

So, I propose a simple, radical act: unplug. Not forever—that’s impractical—but for a while. Find a quiet corner, put the devices away, and look at the world until you see something you haven't noticed before. Watch the way the light hits the wall, or the way the dust dances in a shaft of sun. It isn't a cure for the world's problems, and it won't fix the trajectory of history. But it might just save you. It might help you remember that beneath the digital noise, beneath the frantic pace of the culture, there is still a quiet, steady rhythm to life. And if you listen closely enough, you might find that the rhythm is not moving away from you, but holding you, right here, right now, in the only moment that has ever truly existed.
